title: Chapter 13 - Salvation from Death and Danger

sections.opening: At the opening of this poem called "How long", King David cries from the heart. And sometimes we too have such moments - when we feel that God is distant, that troubles never end, and there is no hope on the horizon. Chapter 13 is the short psalm written precisely for such moments. This is not a chapter of smiles and artificial optimism - this is a chapter of a real person going through a real crisis. And ultimately, finding the way back to the light. In the previous lesson we learned about chapter 12 that protects from evil speech - now we enter the depths of the soul, to the place where pain is sharpest and salvation most needed.
sections.theoryA:
What is the Special Power of Psalms Chapter 13?

Chapter 13 is called "Salvation from Death and Danger" and is considered one of the most powerful psalms for urgent salvation. Its primary power is against mortal danger and any emergency situation requiring immediate divine intervention.

The structure of the chapter is unique - it begins with a cry of complete despair ("How long, O Lord, will You forget me forever") and ends with complete trust ("But I have trusted in Your mercy"). This teaches us that the path to salvation passes through acknowledging pain, not denying it. King David doesn't try to be stronger than he is - he admits before God that he is in despair.

The sages teach that the words "how long" are repeated four times in the chapter, corresponding to the four worlds of Kabbalah - Asiyah, Yetzirah, Beriah, and Atzilut. Each time we say "how long," we touch a different level of the request for salvation.

In Chassidic communities there is a special custom to say chapter 13 when there is a patient in mortal danger. They say it nine times in succession, and each time they mention the patient's name. This is called "nine chapter 13" and is considered one of the strongest powers for healing.

practice1.instruction: Read chapter 13 three times in succession. The first time, focus on the feeling of despair at the beginning of the chapter. The second time, feel how the tone changes in the middle. The third time, notice how the chapter ends in joy and confidence.

sections.theoryB:
How is Psalm 13 Connected to Astrology and Medicine?

In Jewish tradition, each Hebrew month is connected to a specific psalm, and chapter 13 relates to the month of Adar with the sign of Pisces. This connection is not coincidental - Pisces is a sign of deep emotions, the subconscious, and the ability to swim in the murky waters of the soul.

From a Kabbalistic perspective, chapter 13 works primarily on the Sefirah of Malchut - the final Sefirah where divine light descends to the world of action. When we are in a state of "how long," it means our connection to Malchut has weakened, and we feel disconnected from divine providence.

In Hebrew medicine, chapter 13 is considered a psalm that works on the lymphatic system and the heart. The cry "how long" releases accumulated emotional pressure, and at the end of the chapter - "But I have trusted in Your mercy" - returns the heart rate to normal. It's almost like a modern breathing exercise, but with much greater spiritual power.

In Chassidic tradition, chapter 13 is also connected to repairing the covenant - not just the physical covenant, but the covenant between the soul and God. When we say "Answer me, enlighten my eyes" we ask that our spiritual eyes open again to see providence.

practice2.instruction: Write in a notebook the feelings you experience when you say "How long, O Lord, will You forget me forever." Don't think too much - just let the feeling be written. Then write the feeling that "But I have trusted in Your mercy" evokes in you. Compare the two feelings.

sections.deepDive:
What's Not Written in Books

There is a deep secret in chapter 13 that only few know about. The word "Adar" (which is the month of Adar) appears in the gematria of the word "adei" (until when) in the chapter. This is no coincidence - Adar is the month of transforming sorrow to joy, and this chapter does exactly that.

The sages teach that chapter 13 should be said at the time when the sun sets - "between the suns." This is the time when day meets night, light meets darkness. Just like this chapter - where despair meets hope.

There is also a tradition not written anywhere: when someone says chapter 13 in memory of someone who died in a state of despair, it elevates their soul from level to level. The words "lest I sleep the sleep of death" become a prayer not only for life, but for the eternal life of the deceased.

And one final secret: in certain communities there is a custom that when a woman has a difficult birth, they read chapter 13 seven times. The number seven corresponds to the seven voices of David ("the voice of the Lord" that appears seven times in chapter 29), and there is power in this to open any closure.

questions.question: What is the primary power of Psalms Chapter 13?
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 is called 'Salvation from Death and Danger' and is intended for urgent salvation in emergency situations.

questions.question: How many times do the words 'how long' repeat in Chapter 13?
questions.explanation: The words 'how long' repeat four times, corresponding to the four worlds of Kabbalah.

questions.question: To which Hebrew month and zodiac sign does Chapter 13 relate?
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 relates to the month of Adar and the sign of Pisces, connected to deep emotions and the ability to overcome crises.

questions.question: What is the special power called for a patient in mortal danger with Chapter 13?
questions.explanation: The power is called 'Nine Chapter 13' - reading the chapter nine times in succession in memory of the patient.

questions.question: On which Kabbalistic Sefirah does Chapter 13 primarily work?
questions.explanation: Chapter 13 works primarily on the Sefirah of Malchut, where divine light descends to the world of action.

closing.summary: Chapter 13 is the psalm that takes us from the deepest despair to complete trust. It teaches us that it's permitted to be human, to cry out, to be angry at God - and that there, precisely in the most painful place, salvation begins.
closing.wisdom: The path to light passes through darkness, not around it.
closing.nextPreview: In the next lesson we will learn about chapter 14 - how faith conquers heresy, and why David calls heretics 'fools'.

faq.question: When is it recommended to say Psalms Chapter 13?
faq.answer: Chapter 13 is especially recommended in emergency situations, mortal danger, or when feeling deep despair. Ideally said during twilight time.

faq.question: Should Chapter 13 be said a specific number of times?
faq.answer: For urgent salvation, it can be said three times in succession. For a patient in mortal danger there is a custom to say it nine times. There is no obligation for a specific number.

faq.question: Why does Chapter 13 begin with despair and end with joy?
faq.answer: This is the natural structure of true praise - it begins with difficult reality and ends with confidence. God wants us to be honest with Him.

faq.question: Can women say Chapter 13 during childbirth?
faq.answer: Yes, there is an ancient tradition that Chapter 13 helps with difficult birth. It's recommended that someone else read it on behalf of the woman in labor.

seo.title: Psalms Chapter 13 - Salvation from Death and Danger | Powers and Meanings
seo.description: Learn about Psalms Chapter 13 - the psalm for urgent salvation and deliverance from mortal danger. Powers, meanings and ways of recitation for emergency and crisis situations.
